title: How Often Should You Audit Your Suppliers and Sites?
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: How to set GMP audit frequency for suppliers and sites using a risk-based model under EU GMP and ICH Q9 — intervals, triggers and self-inspection explained.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury banner of a calm pharmaceutical quality environment: in soft focus foreground, a gloved hand in a crisp white sleeve rests beside a sleek tablet and a discreet stainless-steel inspection checklist on a polished bench, with rows of capped glass vials blurred behind under shallow depth of field. The palette is cool teal and cyan washed over deep navy shadows, lifted by a single subtle green accent glowing from a status indicator. Soft natural daylight rakes across brushed-metal and glass surfaces, creating gentle reflections and a premium, sterile, trustworthy mood. Composition is uncluttered and editorial, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos, no readable labels.

faqs.q: Is there a legal requirement to audit suppliers every three years?
faqs.a: No. EU GMP does not fix a universal interval; it requires the manufacturing-authorisation holder to assure the quality of materials and outsourced activities using a risk-based approach under ICH Q9. Three years is a common maximum for low-risk, well-performing suppliers, but high-risk relationships often warrant annual or biennial on-site audits. The interval must be justified and documented.

faqs.q: How often must we carry out self-inspections of our own site?
faqs.a: EU GMP Chapter 9 requires self-inspections to monitor compliance, and these are generally expected at least annually across the quality system. You can run one comprehensive audit or a rolling programme, provided every area is covered within the cycle. For GDP operations, the EU GDP guidelines similarly require self-inspections at a defined, appropriate frequency.

faqs.q: When should we bring a supplier audit forward?
faqs.a: Schedule a for-cause audit whenever risk signals appear: a significant deviation or complaint trend, a recall or regulatory action, a major change such as a new site or process transfer, adverse regulatory intelligence, or repeated failure to close CAPAs on time. A genuinely risk-based programme reacts to these triggers rather than waiting for the next calendar date.
